title: Local- and regional-scale air pollution modelling (PM(10)) and exposure assessment for pregnancy trimesters, infancy, and childhood to age 15 years: Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents And Children (ALSPAC).
ehr_evidence: The study uses the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents And Children (ALSPAC) birth cohort and residential address history, not electronic health records.
summary: The study establishes air pollution modelling (PM10) to assess exposures during pregnancy, infancy, and childhood (up to age 15) for the ALSPAC birth cohort using local and regional/long-range models.
key_findings: PM10 exposures fell from 31.2 μg/m³ at age 1 to 19.6 μg/m³ at age 15.
Local sources contributed 18.5%–19.5% to total PM10 during pregnancy and infancy.
Main roads contributed approximately 3.0% to total PM10 exposures across all periods.
population: Children and mothers in the ALSPAC birth cohort (pregnancy to age ~15)
limitations: Lack of fixed-site PM10 monitoring during pregnancy
Reliance on dispersion modelling for historic sub-annual periods
